section.title: The Palace and the Temple Furnishings
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 6 closed with the temple finished in seven years. Chapter 7 turns first to Solomon's own household, a building program that takes thirteen years, longer than the house of the LORD itself, before returning to the temple to catalogue in careful detail the furnishings Huram of Tyre casts in bronze and the vessels Solomon makes in gold: the two great pillars named Jachin and Boaz, the molten Sea resting on twelve oxen, the ten wheeled stands and basins, and the golden altar, table, lampstands, and utensils. This long inventory is the necessary preparation for the ark's arrival and the glory of the LORD filling the house in chapter 8.
section.thesis: Solomon's own sprawling palace complex stands beside the temple as a reminder of the king's real but lesser glory, while the exacting skill and abundant costliness poured into the bronze and gold furnishings for the LORD's house show that true wealth and craftsmanship are rightly directed first toward the worship of God.
section.frameNote: Five units: Solomon's palace complex, verses 1 to 12; the bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz, verses 13 to 22; the bronze Sea, verses 23 to 26; the ten stands and basins, verses 27 to 39; and the completion of the bronze and gold furnishings, verses 40 to 51.

verses.text: Solomon, however, took thirteen years to complete the construction of his entire {{bayit|palace}}.
He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon a hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high, with four rows of cedar pillars supporting the cedar beams.
The house was roofed with cedar above the beams that rested on the pillars, forty-five beams, fifteen per row.
There were three rows of high windows facing one another in three tiers.
All the doorways had rectangular frames, with the openings facing one another in three tiers.
Solomon made his colonnade fifty cubits long and thirty cubits wide, with a portico in front of it and a canopy with pillars in front of the portico.
In addition, he built a hall for the {{kisse|throne}}, the Hall of Justice, where he was to judge. It was paneled with cedar from floor to ceiling.
And the palace where Solomon would live, set further back, was of similar construction. He also made a palace like this hall for Pharaoh's daughter, whom he had married.
All these buildings were constructed with costly stones, cut to size and trimmed with saws inside and out from the foundation to the eaves, and from the outside to the great courtyard.
The foundations were laid with large, costly stones, some ten cubits long and some eight cubits long.
Above these were costly stones, cut to size, and cedar beams.
The great courtyard was surrounded by three rows of dressed stone and a row of trimmed cedar beams, as were the inner courtyard and portico of the house of the LORD.
Now King Solomon sent to bring Huram from Tyre.

He was the son of a widow from the tribe of Naphtali, and his father was a man of Tyre, a craftsman in {{nechoshet|bronze}}. Huram had great skill, understanding, and knowledge for every kind of bronze work. So he came to King Solomon and carried out all his work.
He cast two pillars of bronze, each eighteen cubits high and twelve cubits in circumference.
He also made two capitals of cast bronze to set on top of the pillars, each capital five cubits high.
For the capitals on top of the pillars he made a network of lattice, with wreaths of chainwork, seven for each capital.
Likewise, he made the pillars with two rows of pomegranates around each grating to cover each capital atop the pillars.
And the capitals atop the pillars in the portico were shaped like lilies, four cubits high.
On the capitals of both pillars, just above the rounded projection next to the network, were the two hundred pomegranates in rows encircling each capital.
Thus he set up the pillars at the portico of the temple. The pillar to the south he named Jachin, and the pillar to the north he named Boaz.
And the tops of the pillars were shaped like lilies. So the work of the pillars was completed.
He also made the {{yam|Sea}} of cast metal. It was circular in shape, measuring ten cubits from rim to rim, five cubits in height, and thirty cubits in circumference.
Below the rim, ornamental buds encircled it, ten per cubit all the way around the Sea, cast in two rows as a part of the Sea.
The Sea stood on twelve oxen, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east. The Sea rested on them, with all their hindquarters toward the center.
It was a handbreadth thick, and its rim was fashioned like the brim of a cup, like a lily blossom. It could hold two thousand baths.
In addition, he made ten movable stands of bronze, each four cubits long, four cubits wide, and three cubits high.
This was the design of the stands: They had side panels attached to uprights,
and on the panels between the uprights were lions, oxen, and cherubim. On the uprights was a pedestal above, and below the lions and oxen were wreaths of beveled work.
Each stand had four bronze wheels with bronze axles and a basin resting on four supports, with wreaths at each side.

The opening to each stand inside the crown at the top was one cubit deep, with a round opening like the design of a pedestal, a cubit and a half wide. And around its opening were engravings, but the panels of the stands were square, not round.
There were four wheels under the panels, and the axles of the wheels were attached to the stand; each wheel was a cubit and a half in diameter.
The wheels were made like chariot wheels; their axles, rims, spokes, and hubs were all of cast metal.
Each stand had four handles, one for each corner, projecting from the stand.
At the top of each stand was a circular band half a cubit high. The supports and panels were cast as a unit with the top of the stand.
He engraved cherubim, lions, and palm trees on the surfaces of the supports and panels, wherever each had space, with wreaths all around.
In this way he made the ten stands, each with the same casting, dimensions, and shape.
He also made ten bronze basins, each holding forty baths and measuring four cubits across, one basin for each of the ten stands.
He set five stands on the south side of the temple and five on the north, and he put the Sea on the south side, at the southeast corner of the temple.

units.label: The bronze and gold furnishings completed
units.note: everything for the house of the LORD

verses.text: Additionally, Huram made the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. So Huram finished all the work that he had undertaken for King Solomon in the {{bayit|house}} of the LORD:
the two pillars; the two bowl-shaped capitals atop the pillars; the two sets of network covering both bowls of the capitals atop the pillars;
the four hundred pomegranates for the two sets of network (two rows of pomegranates for each network covering both the bowl-shaped capitals atop the pillars);
the ten stands; the ten basins on the stands;
the Sea; the twelve oxen underneath the Sea;
and the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. All the articles that Huram made for King Solomon in the house of the LORD were made of burnished bronze.
The king had them cast in clay molds in the plain of the Jordan between Succoth and Zarethan.
Solomon left all these articles unweighed, because there were so many. The weight of the bronze could not be determined.
Solomon also made all the furnishings for the house of the LORD: the golden altar; the golden table on which was placed the Bread of the Presence;
the lampstands of pure gold in front of the inner sanctuary, five on the right side and five on the left; the gold flowers, lamps, and tongs;

the pure gold basins, wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, ladles, and censers; and the gold hinges for the doors of the inner temple (that is, the Most Holy Place) as well as for the doors of the main hall of the temple.

So all the work that King Solomon had performed for the house of the LORD was completed. Then Solomon brought in the items his father David had dedicated, the silver, the gold, and the furnishings, and he placed them in the treasuries of the house of the LORD.

bayit.gloss: House, household, palace, or temple. The same word covers Solomon's own palace and the house of the LORD, so the chapter's repeated use of bayit quietly invites comparison between the king's house and God's house, a comparison the narrator has already made pointed by noting Solomon spent thirteen years on his own bayit against seven on the LORD's.

kisse.gloss: Throne, seat of authority. Solomon builds a hall for his kisse where he judges Israel, a visible sign of the reign the LORD granted him; every earthly kisse in Israel's story is measured against the greater throne God has promised David's line forever, 2 Samuel 7:16.

nechoshet.gloss: Bronze or copper. The metal of Huram's craft, cast in such quantity that Solomon leaves it unweighed, verse 47; bronze furnished the outer, more publicly accessible vessels of temple worship, in contrast to the gold reserved for the inner sanctuary and its closest furnishings.

yam.gloss: Sea. The name given to the great bronze basin resting on twelve oxen, used for the priests' washing, 2 Chronicles 4:6; its vast capacity and cosmic name evoke the primeval waters tamed and put to holy use in the service of the LORD who made and rules the sea, Psalm 104:6-9.

basic.context: The temple itself took Solomon seven years, chapter 6, but this chapter reveals his own palace complex took thirteen, nearly twice as long. Before returning to the temple's furnishings, the narrator lingers over Solomon's residence, his hall of judgment, and a matching house for Pharaoh's daughter. Then attention shifts to Huram, a skilled bronze worker from Tyre, who casts the two great pillars at the temple porch, the enormous bronze Sea resting on twelve oxen, and ten wheeled stands with their basins. The chapter closes by cataloguing the gold furnishings, the altar, table, lampstands, and utensils, that Solomon himself provided for the house of the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific building project, not a pattern prescribing how every place of worship should be built or furnished today; the temple's design was directly revealed to David, 1 Chronicles 28:19, for that particular house in redemptive history. What endures is the underlying principle the details illustrate: skill, cost, and care are fitting offerings to God, and the narrator's quiet comparison between the king's palace and the LORD's house invites reflection on where our own greatest investment of time and resources actually goes.
basic.originalLanguages: The word bayit, house, binds the chapter together, describing both Solomon's own extensive palace and the house of the LORD, inviting a comparison the numbers themselves already make. Huram works in nechoshet, bronze, casting the great yam, the Sea, whose watery vastness recalls the primeval deep now tamed for holy use, while Solomon's hall for his kisse, throne, shows a king who judges under, and is himself under, the greater reign of God.
basic.typology: The bronze Sea, so vast that priests could wash there before serving, points forward to the greater cleansing found in Christ, who washes His people once for all, Titus 3:5; the two pillars, Jachin, He establishes, and Boaz, in Him is strength, together confess that Israel's stability and strength rest not in Solomon's engineering but in the LORD who establishes and empowers His people, a truth fulfilled in Christ the true foundation and strength of His church, 1 Corinthians 3:11.
basic.christ: The golden lampstands, altar, and table of the Bread of the Presence anticipate realities fulfilled in Jesus: the light of the world, John 8:12; the true bread from heaven, John 6:35; and the once for all sacrifice at the true altar of the cross, Hebrews 13:10. The pillar names, Jachin and Boaz, preach in miniature the gospel confession that only God establishes and strengthens His people, a confession answered fully in Christ, in whom all the fullness of God dwells bodily, Colossians 2:9, the true and greater temple, John 2:19-21.
basic.god: God is honored through abundant, skillful, costly work devoted to His worship, yet the chapter also quietly relativizes even Solomon's greatest achievements by noting that his own palace outpaced the temple in time and, by implication, in personal priority; God is not impressed by scale alone but receives the careful, willing offering of skill and wealth that flows from a heart devoted to Him, a devotion this chapter shows beginning to divide even in Solomon.
basic.crossReferences: First Chronicles 28 records that David gave Solomon the plan for the temple by the Spirit, showing the furnishings were not human invention but divinely patterned. Second Chronicles 4 parallels this chapter's inventory of the bronze and gold. John 2:19 to 21 identifies Jesus' own body as the true temple. And Revelation 21:22 declares that in the new creation there is no temple, for the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple, the final answer to every earthly house built for God's Name.
basic.oneStory: Israel's kingdom reaches its material and architectural height in this chapter, yet even here the narrative plants a quiet warning: Solomon spends longer building for himself than he did building for the LORD. The lavish bronze and gold furnishings point forward to a greater house, the temple of Christ's own body, and a greater dwelling still to come, when God's presence will fill the new creation with no need of any handmade temple at all.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as an inventory, moving from Solomon's palace complex, verses 1 to 12, into a detailed technical description of Huram's bronze work, verses 13 to 45, and closing with a shorter list of gold furnishings, verses 46 to 51. The narrator's precision, measurements, materials, and craftsmanship, mirrors the exacting detail given for the tabernacle in Exodus, signaling that this house, like that one, is built exactly as God intended, while the placement of the palace account before the furnishings list creates a literary comparison the numbers themselves reinforce.
context.historical: Huram of Tyre represents the same Phoenician expertise in bronze casting that supplied Hiram king of Tyre's cedar in chapter 5; Tyre was renowned throughout the ancient Near East for its metalworking. The described dimensions and techniques, lost wax casting in clay molds in the Jordan valley's clay-rich soil, verse 46, match known ancient Near Eastern bronze casting methods, and comparable large basins and ornamented stands appear in archaeological finds from neighboring cultures, showing Solomon's temple furnishings, while unique in purpose, employed the finest craftsmanship of his day.
context.canonical: This chapter completes the material preparation begun with David's stockpiling, 1 Chronicles 22, and Solomon's building in chapter 6, setting the stage for the ark's entrance and the glory of the LORD in chapter 8. The bronze Sea and its priestly washing anticipate the greater cleansing of the New Covenant, Hebrews 10:22, and the whole furnished house anticipates its own fulfillment and surpassing in Christ, the true temple, John 2:21, and in the new creation, where no temple is needed at all, Revelation 21:22.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record, with the same careful precision given to the tabernacle in Exodus, the completion of Solomon's building projects, showing both the glory of a well-furnished house for the LORD and, through the pointed detail that Solomon's own palace took nearly twice as long as the temple, a subtle warning about where the king's deepest investment was beginning to go.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a one time, divinely patterned building project; it does not prescribe particular architectural forms, materials, or proportions for worship spaces today. What is prescriptive is the underlying value the chapter commends: that skill, wealth, and care belong first to the honor of God, and that believers should examine whether their own greatest investments mirror Solomon's priorities or correct them.

debates.question: Is the narrator's mention that Solomon's palace took thirteen years, against seven for the temple, meant as criticism?

views.label: A deliberate, quiet warning
views.case: The narrator elsewhere in 1 Kings is not shy about direct criticism, yet here simply places the numbers side by side without comment, a technique used throughout Kings to let facts convict; combined with the later notice that Solomon built an even grander palace for Pharaoh's daughter, verse 8, and the mounting concerns about foreign wives and wealth in chapters 10 and 11, the juxtaposition reads as an early, understated signal of priorities beginning to shift.

views.label: Simply a factual, non-evaluative record
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern royal building projects commonly took many years, and a king's residence, throne hall, and household buildings naturally required more square footage and rooms than a single temple structure; the text may simply be recording construction facts without implying moral commentary, especially since Solomon is not yet under explicit divine rebuke at this point in the narrative.

debates.bearing: Either reading leaves the reader to weigh, in light of Solomon's later decline in chapter 11, whether the seeds of divided devotion were already present in chapter 7's building order, and both readings agree that the temple furnishings themselves are described with unqualified honor.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key word is bayit, house, used of both Solomon's own residence and the house of the LORD, a repetition that lets the reader measure one against the other without the narrator needing to say so directly. Huram, master of nechoshet, bronze, casts the yam, the Sea, whose name recalls the untamed waters of ancient near eastern mythology, here fully domesticated to serve the washing of God's priests. Solomon's hall for his kisse, throne, situates his own rule for judging Israel, a reign that is itself accountable to the greater King whose house stands nearby.

typology.type: The bronze Sea for priestly washing
typology.fulfillment: The greater washing of regeneration Christ provides for His people, Titus 3:5, and the full assurance of a heart sprinkled clean and a body washed with pure water, Hebrews 10:22.

typology.type: The pillars Jachin, He establishes, and Boaz, in Him is strength
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sure foundation who establishes His church, 1 Corinthians 3:11, and the strength in whom believers stand, Philippians 4:13, Ephesians 6:10.

typology.type: The golden lampstands, table, and altar
typology.fulfillment: Jesus as the light of the world, John 8:12, the bread of life, John 6:35, and the once for all sacrifice offered at the true altar, Hebrews 13:10-12.

study.christ: The furnishings Huram and Solomon craft are shadows of realities fulfilled in Christ: the lampstands anticipate Jesus, the light of the world, John 8:12; the table of the Bread of the Presence anticipates the true bread from heaven, John 6:35; and the altar anticipates the once for all sacrifice at the cross, Hebrews 13:10-12. Even the pillar names preach the gospel in miniature, Jachin, He establishes, and Boaz, in Him is strength, confessing that Israel's stability rests not in Solomon's skill but in the LORD, a confession answered fully in Christ, the sure foundation, 1 Corinthians 3:11, in whom all the fullness of deity dwells bodily, Colossians 2:9, the greater temple who was destroyed and raised in three days, John 2:19-21.
study.god: God is honored by excellence, skill, and abundance devoted to His worship, seen in the meticulous, costly craftsmanship of the bronze and gold furnishings. Yet the same chapter, by its plain report that Solomon's own palace took nearly twice as long to build as the temple, quietly shows that God is not fooled or flattered by outward magnificence alone; He sees whether the greater share of a king's, or anyone's, time, wealth, and devotion is actually given to Him. The chapter thus holds together God's delight in beautiful, careful worship and His discernment of divided hearts.

crossReferences.note: David gives Solomon the divinely revealed plan for the temple and its furnishings, showing their design was not human invention.
The parallel Chronicles account of the bronze and gold furnishings, confirming and supplementing the details in this chapter.
Jesus identifies His own body as the temple, the ultimate fulfillment of the house Solomon built and furnished.
The greater cleansing and confidence believers have in Christ, of which the priestly washing at the bronze Sea was a shadow.
In the new creation there is no temple, for the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple, the final answer to every house built for God's Name.

study.oneStory: At the height of Israel's united kingdom, Solomon completes not only the temple but a vast palace complex for himself, and Huram of Tyre fills the LORD's house with bronze and gold furnishings of extraordinary craftsmanship. The narrator's quiet comparison, thirteen years for the king's house against seven for God's, plants a seed that will grow into full-blown compromise by chapter 11. Every lampstand, table, and altar points forward to Christ, the true light, bread, and sacrifice, and to the temple not made with hands, whose greater glory will one day need no gold or bronze at all, Revelation 21:22.

quiz.q: How long did Solomon's own palace complex take to build, compared to the temple?

options.text: Thirteen years, nearly twice as long as the seven years spent on the temple, v1.
options.note: Verse 1 states thirteen years for the palace, against the seven years given for the temple in 6:38.

options.text: Seven years, the same length of time as the temple.
options.note: This confuses the palace timeline with the temple's, understating the contrast the text draws.

options.text: Twenty years total for both projects combined, as stated later in chapter 9.
options.note: Twenty years, 9:10, is the combined total for both buildings, not the palace alone; it does not answer what this verse states about the palace by itself.

options.text: The text gives no timeframe for Solomon's palace.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly states thirteen years.

quiz.q: What were the two bronze pillars at the temple portico named, and what do the names likely mean?

options.text: Jachin, probably meaning He establishes, and Boaz, probably meaning in Him is strength, v21.
options.note: Verse 21 and the BSB footnotes give these names and their probable meanings.

options.text: Cherubim and Seraphim, representing the angelic guardians of the temple.
options.note: Cherubim are engraved on the stands, verse 29, but the pillars themselves are named Jachin and Boaz, not named for angelic beings.

options.text: Solomon and David, honoring the king and his father.
options.note: The pillars are not named after the kings; their names carry theological meaning about God's establishing power and strength.

options.text: The pillars were left unnamed in the biblical text.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly names them Jachin and Boaz.

quiz.q: What was the bronze Sea used for, and what did it rest upon?

options.text: A massive basin for priestly washing, resting on twelve bronze oxen facing outward in four groups of three, v23-25.
options.note: Verses 23-25 describe its construction on twelve oxen; 2 Chronicles 4:6 confirms its washing function.

options.text: A decorative pool with no functional use in temple worship.
options.note: It was not merely decorative; alongside its symbolic grandeur it served a practical washing function for the priests.

options.text: A container for storing the temple's gold and silver treasures.
options.note: The treasuries, not the Sea, held David's dedicated silver and gold, verse 51; the Sea held water.

options.text: It rested directly on the ground with no supporting structure.
options.note: Verse 25 specifically describes it resting on twelve bronze oxen.

quiz.q: Why does Solomon leave the bronze articles unweighed, verse 47?

options.text: Because there was so much bronze that its weight could not be determined, showing the sheer abundance devoted to the LORD's house.
options.note: Verse 47 gives this exact reason, underscoring the lavish scale of the offering.

options.text: Because Solomon wanted to hide the true cost of the temple from the people.
options.note: The text gives no hint of concealment; the point is abundance, not secrecy.

options.text: Because bronze was considered worthless and not worth counting.
options.note: Bronze was a valued, skillfully worked metal throughout the chapter, not worthless material.

options.text: Because Huram refused to allow the articles to be weighed.
options.note: The text gives no such refusal; Solomon simply left them unweighed due to their vast quantity.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the placement of the bronze furnishings from the gold furnishings in the temple?

options.text: The bronze pillars, Sea, and stands stood in the courtyard for public and priestly use, while the gold altar, table, and lampstands furnished the inner house nearer the Most Holy Place, v39, 48-50.
options.note: The chapter's structure and verses 39 and 48-50 show bronze in the courtyard and gold within the temple's interior.

options.text: All the furnishings, bronze and gold alike, were placed together in the outer courtyard.
options.note: This flattens the text's clear distinction between courtyard bronze and interior gold furnishings.

options.text: The bronze furnishings were placed inside the Most Holy Place itself.
options.note: The Most Holy Place held only the ark, 8:9; the courtyard, not the inner sanctuary, held the bronze works.

options.text: There is no meaningful distinction; bronze and gold were interchangeable throughout the temple.
options.note: The chapter's own arrangement, courtyard bronze followed by interior gold furnishings, shows a clear, purposeful distinction.

questions: The narrator quietly notes that Solomon's own palace took nearly twice as long to build as the temple. Where might your own time, money, or attention be quietly favoring your own house over devotion to God?
Huram poured extraordinary skill into bronze work that would serve the LORD's house. How can you offer your own particular skills and craft as an act of worship this week?

The pillars Jachin and Boaz confess that Israel's strength and stability come from God, not human achievement. Where are you tempted to lean on your own competence rather than on the LORD who establishes and strengthens?
The bronze Sea provided washing for the priests before they served. How does knowing that Christ has washed you completely change the way you approach serving Him today?
Solomon left the bronze unweighed because there was simply too much to count. What would it look like for you to give to God's work with the same unmeasured generosity?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote estimates the House of the Forest of Lebanon at roughly 150 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high, giving a sense of the massive scale of Solomon's own building projects.
The BSB footnote clarifies that Hebrew Hiram is a variant of Huram, the craftsman from Tyre; this is a different individual from Hiram king of Tyre mentioned in 5:1.

The BSB footnotes explain Jachin probably means He establishes and Boaz probably means in Him is strength, names that turn the temple's architecture into a quiet confession about the source of Israel's stability.
The BSB footnote notes the LXX does not include the sentence about the Sea's capacity of two thousand baths, a minor textual variant that does not affect the chapter's description of its grandeur.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 7:1-51. All em dashes and en dashes present in the source (for example in verses 3, 40-45, 48-50) were converted to commas or clean breaks; none remain anywhere in this file.
